ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GG (Rice dishes) is the closest defensible match. No specific belacan fried rice code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for balachan rice or belacan fried rice. SNOMED CT food coding is limited for Southeast Asian prepared dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of balachan rice (no meat) is approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve lunch or dinner portion as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Balachan Rice (No Meat) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Balachan Rice (No Meat), cr_id G00010609, with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input record. All per-100g values are preserved from the HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for balachan fried rice. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain fried rice (white rice base, GI ~64, medium category). Belacan, egg, chilli, and spring onion additions are low-carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (64) × available carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 250 g serving = 62.975 g) ÷ 100 = 40.3 g GL. This is a high GL per serving, reflecting the large rice portion; portion control is clinically important.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio favourable at ~0.71)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is in the borderline-elevated range for hypertension management; patients should limit portion size to 150–200 g, avoid adding soy sauce or fish sauce, and request minimal shrimp paste to keep total sodium intake within the 1500–2000 mg/day DASH target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g with medium GI (~64); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g moderates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI (~64) and moderate carbohydrate content produce a high glycemic load per standard serving (GL ~40); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150 g or less, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and avoid sweet condiments to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 1.2 g/100g; cholesterol 0 mg/100g; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g supports lipid management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is low (~1.2 g/100g) and cholesterol is negligible (0 mg/100g), making this dish acceptable for cardiovascular disease patients in moderate portions; the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre contributes to LDL reduction, but sodium at 231 mg/100g warrants portion control to avoid exceeding daily sodium targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chilli (nightshade) present; solanine may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should request a chilli-free version of this dish and consider adding anti-inflammatory herbs such as turmeric or ginger to offset the absence of positive anti-inflammatory factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Balachan Rice (No Meat) is a Singaporean hawker dish consisting of stir-fried white rice seasoned with belacan (fermented shrimp paste), chilli, garlic, spring onion, and egg, prepared without any meat. The dish is cooked in a wok over high heat, giving the rice a characteristic smoky, umami-rich flavour with a pungent, savoury depth from the shrimp paste and a mild heat from chilli. The texture is fluffy yet slightly sticky, with egg providing richness. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat (saturated fat approximately 1.2 g), 25.19 g carbohydrate, 0.21 g sugar, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The dish is a moderate-GI food (estimated GI ~64) with a high glycemic load per standard 250 g serving (GL ~40), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (231 mg Na vs 327 mg K per 100 g) partially mitigates hypertension risk, though total sodium intake should be monitored. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner at Singaporean hawker centres and is culturally associated with the Burmese-Singaporean community. The Myanmar-language name ငပိထမင်းနယ် refers to the shrimp paste rice preparation style.
common_user_queries: Calories in balachan rice no meat

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010609) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database, plain fried rice) with documented rationale.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific balachan rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (G00010609) as of 2026-04-16. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; the no-meat version with egg contributes negligible cholesterol at the reported level). 3. saturated_fat_g of 1.2 g/100g is estimated from the health_suitability input field reference (~1.2 g/100g) consistent with the total fat of 4.21 g/100g for a stir-fried rice dish with egg; this is an internal estimate and should be confirmed against the full HPB nutrient panel when available. 4. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain fried rice, white rice base); no direct GI measurement for balachan fried rice was found in approved sources. 5. The dish name includes a Myanmar-script component (ငပိထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်) reflecting the Burmese-Singaporean community origin of this hawker item. 6. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
